active_paradigms.idea: Selective inhibitor suppression of only the autocatalytic elongation channel (leave seed/primary nucleation uninhibited), using bounded Langmuir occupancy
active_paradigms.description: Brichos/secondary-nucleation biology shows inhibitors bind fibril surfaces and preferentially shut down the AUTOCATALYTIC (surface-catalyzed, c-dependent) channel while primary and seed-templated initiation are largely untouched. Restructure the RHS so suppression multiplies ONLY the elongation term: expression = (P - c) * (seed_nuc + primary_nuc + suppression * elongation), where suppression = 1/(1 + c3^2*theta) and theta = inhibitor/(c5^2 + inhibitor). This changes the FUNCTIONAL FORM of how cd delays curves: at high dose the c-driven burst is throttled but the slow nucleation floor still eventually reaches plateau, which stretches the lag phase and shifts the half-time in a dose-dependent way. This directly targets the shape_loss timing/slope term (25% weight) differently from all failed capacity-shape edits, because it modulates the slope PROFILE (steep autocatalytic burst vs slow floor) rather than the state-capacity shape. Keep 6 squared constants, same feature scaling (1e6). cd=0 gives theta=0, suppression=1, exact uninhibited limit preserved. Use initial_values=[0.5,0.05,0.5,1.0,0.92,0.3].
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: heldout_curve_shape_loss (slope profile + half-time timing) while holding NMSE near current; aggregate scored_loss
active_paradigms.cautions: Ensure elongation still uses monomer*concentration so the burst is autocatalytic; verify plateau P=c[4] stays near 0.92 and capacity (P-c) never inverts sign during integration. Denominator stays >=1 so no singularity.

active_paradigms.idea: Reduce to 5 constants and improve least-squares conditioning via balanced feature scaling and physically motivated initial_values to fix constant-fitting reliability across noisy/small datasets
active_paradigms.description: No prior attempt targeted the over-parameterization / fit-robustness failure mode, yet the aggregate penalizes any dataset that fits poorly. Merge primary_nucleation and seed_nucleation are both linear source terms; on many curves M0 and m0 are collinear per-curve constants, so one of the two squared constants is weakly identifiable and destabilizes least squares. Collapse to a single combined source coefficient acting on (a*seed + monomer) with one shared scale, i.e. source = c0^2*(c1^2*seed + monomer), giving 5 constants total: source scale, seed/monomer ratio, elongation c2, suppression strength c3, and Kd c5, with plateau fixed structurally by using normalized capacity (1 - c) instead of a free plateau (data is globally min-max normalized so the plateau is ~1). expression = suppression*(1 - concentration)*(source + c2^2*monomer*concentration). Fewer constants make the single-start fit more reliable on small/noisy datasets, reducing the chance any dataset blows up NMSE and drags the mean. Set initial_values to order-one magnitudes matching the 1e6-scaled features: [0.4, 0.3, 0.6, 1.0, 0.3].
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: worst-case per-dataset NMSE robustness and aggregate scored_loss by making constant fitting more identifiable; avoid the penalty from any single failing dataset
active_paradigms.cautions: Fixing plateau at 1.0 assumes data reaches full normalized range; verify no curve plateaus well below 1 causing systematic bias. If NMSE regresses broadly, the free plateau was load-bearing and should be restored as c4.

active_paradigms.idea: Self-limiting autocatalytic feedback using product-form growth c*(P-c) applied only to elongation, giving intrinsic sigmoidal lag-burst-plateau asymmetry driven by state feedback rather than a capacity-power
active_paradigms.description: Prior failures altered capacity shape via powers/logs on (P-c); instead introduce a distinct logistic-in-feedback structure where the autocatalytic term itself carries the c-multiplier producing the classic burst, while the nucleation floor provides the initiating lag. expression = suppression*( (P - c)*(seed_nuc + primary_nuc) + c2^2*monomer*concentration*(P - concentration) ). Here the elongation term c*(P-c) is the standard Fisher/logistic autocatalysis (peaks at c=P/2), creating an asymmetric front that is slow at the start (small c), fast mid-curve, and self-arresting at plateau — precisely the amyloid lag-burst-plateau slope profile the shape_loss measures. Because the two channels have DIFFERENT capacity structure, their relative fitted weights let the model tune half-time and slope steepness per dataset via shared constants, targeting the timing term without adding fragile exponents. Keep suppression=1/(1+c3^2*theta), theta=inhibitor/(c5^2+inhibitor), plateau P=c4, 6 squared constants, 1e6 scaling, initial_values=[0.5,0.05,0.6,1.0,0.92,0.3].
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: shape_loss slope-profile and half-time components via asymmetric self-limiting feedback; keep NMSE stable
active_paradigms.cautions: Ensure both (P-c) and (P-concentration) use the same plateau to avoid double-counting; the product term stays bounded because c in [0,P] so c*(P-c)>=0. Watch that the extra product doesn't slow onset too much on uninhibited curves — the linear nucleation channel must remain to seed the initial rise.

tried_paradigms.idea: Explicit lag-phase via time-delayed source coupling: inhibitor shifts onset time additively rather than only scaling rate
tried_paradigms.description: Introduce a lag/onset mechanism where the effective driving source is gated by an accumulating self-limiting factor of the current state, and let cd raise the activation threshold. Structure: growth = capacity * (seed_nuc + primary_nuc + elongation) where elongation ~ c2^2*monomer*c, and multiply the autocatalytic buildup by a smooth self-accelerating factor (c + c6^2) representing minimal nucleated mass, divided by inhibitor threshold shift: expression = capacity*( c0^2*seed + c1^2*monomer + c2^2*monomer*c*(c + c6^2) ) / (1 + c3^2*inhibitor). The (c + c6^2) factor makes elongation start slow (small c) and accelerate — a physically grounded latency that sharpens the sigmoid onset without needing c**2 explicitly, and the small offset c6^2 keeps a nonzero seed of growth. This directly attacks the shape/onset timing term by producing a genuine lag. plateau=c4, capacity=plateau-c. 7 constants, all squared/positive; denominator>=1. cd=0 recovers uninhibited kinetics. initial_values=[0.5,0.05,0.5,1.0,0.92,0.3,0.1].
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: dataset_shape_loss onset/half-response timing via genuine lag phase from state-dependent autocatalytic activation
tried_paradigms.cautions: Keep c6^2 init small (0.1) so the lag is expressed but growth still initiates from seed/primary terms; verify c stays in [0,1] so (c+c6^2) bounded. Ensure the product term does not create numerical stiffness at large capacity — plateau near 0.92-1.0 keeps it moderate. 7 constants under limit; watch for redundancy between primary_nuc and the offset c6^2.

tried_paradigms.idea: Multiplicative cooperative capacity: replace linear (P-c) with (P-c)*(a+c) autocatalytic saturating factor to produce a true Cohen-Knowles sigmoidal lag-burst-plateau, directly targeting shape_loss
tried_paradigms.description: The current linear capacity (P-c) yields near-symmetric logistic kinetics with a weak lag phase, which the shape_loss (slope-profile + onset timing) penalizes. From the amyloid master equation, aggregate mass grows as m(t)~cosh-type with an explicit exponential lag governed by autocatalytic feedback proportional to existing aggregate. Encode this by making growth self-reinforcing near c=0 yet capped near c=P: set expression = (plateau - concentration) * (c_small + concentration) * (k0^2*seed + k1^2*monomer) / inhibitor_scale, where c_small = c[6]^2 is a small positive nucleation floor keeping dc/dt>0 at c=0 (avoids a stuck fixed point) and (a+c) supplies quadratic autocatalysis that sharpens the burst and lengthens the lag. Keep the existing saturable inhibitor_scale = 1 + c[3]^2*inhibitor^2/(c[5]^2+inhibitor) and squared constants for sign-definiteness. This uses one extra constant (7 total) but converts symmetric growth into asymmetric sigmoid without a fragile Gompertz log. Keep initial_values with a small floor like 0.02 for c[6]. The product (P-c)*(a+c) is a smooth quadratic, globally bounded, no singularities.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: dataset_shape_loss (slope profile and half-response/onset timing) while preserving nmse; improves heldout_curve_shape_loss which is the primary generalization diagnostic
tried_paradigms.cautions: Do not let a+c degenerate: keep c_small>0 via squared constant so dc/dt is nonzero at c=0. Ensure plateau initial value ~0.92 and c_small init small (~0.03). Watch that the quadratic in c does not overshoot plateau during odeint (it cannot since (P-c) forces the derivative to 0 at c=P). Keep total constants under 13 (7 used).

tried_paradigms.idea: Time-gated effective rate: multiply the growth rate by a smooth saturating time ramp x0/(tau+x0) so the model represents an emergent nucleation lag whose onset shifts with inhibitor, directly matching half-time timing in shape_loss
tried_paradigms.description: Rather than reshaping capacity, make the effective aggregation rate ramp in over normalized time so the flat lag phase is explicit. amyloid lag time scales with nucleation barriers and is pushed later by inhibitors. Define time_gate = x0^2 / (tau^2 + x0^2) with tau = c[6]^2 (a smooth Hill-in-time, 0 at t=0 rising to 1), so early derivative is suppressed producing a genuine lag, then growth engages. Fold the inhibitor into the lag onset by setting tau_eff shifted by inhibitor: tau2 = c[5]^2 + c[6]^2*inhibitor, then time_gate = x0^2/(tau2 + x0^2). Full RHS = (plateau - concentration) * (k0^2*seed + k1^2*monomer + k2^2*monomer*concentration) * time_gate. Because inhibitor now delays the gate onset (later half-time) rather than merely scaling amplitude, it captures the observed t_half shift (0.077->0.14) as a pure timing effect, which is exactly what shape_loss measures. All denominators strictly positive (squared constants + nonneg inhibitor). x0 is min-max normalized to [0,1] so x0^2 is well conditioned. Uses 7 constants.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: half-response/onset timing component of shape_loss and heldout extrapolation across inhibitor doses; keeps nmse via retained elongation term
tried_paradigms.cautions: x0 is normalized per-curve so t=0 truly gives gate=0; ensure initial_values give tau^2 around 0.01-0.05 so the lag is short enough not to kill fast curves. Avoid making the gate too steep (large tau) which flattens everything. Since dc/dt->0 as x0->0, the seed/primary nucleation still needs enough weight for seeded curves that start rising immediately; keep c2 elongation to preserve autocatalysis.

tried_paradigms.idea: Fractional-order autocatalytic capacity using a stable power sqrt/exponent-free surrogate: capacity (P-c) raised to a fitted-but-bounded cooperativity via (P-c)*(P-c+eps) and monomer-consumption coupling to enforce mass conservation between free monomer and aggregate
tried_paradigms.description: Impose true mass conservation: free monomer depletes as aggregate grows, so the elongation driving force should be (m0-based available monomer) not fixed m0. Model available monomer as (monomer_scaled * (plateau - concentration)) i.e. monomer is consumed proportionally to converted mass, coupling growth to a shrinking substrate pool and naturally producing the burst-then-decelerate asymmetry. RHS = (plateau - concentration) * (k0^2*seed + k1^2*monomer*(plateau-concentration) + k2^2*monomer*concentration*(plateau-concentration)) / inhibitor_scale. This makes the primary-nucleation and elongation channels doubly capacity-gated (mass-conserving substrate depletion), giving a sharper sigmoid with a longer lag and faster late saturation than a single (P-c) factor, without any log/power/exp that risks non-real values. Keep the saturable inhibitor_scale unchanged and squared constants. This is a first-principles conservation change (substrate + product = const) rather than a phenomenological reshape. Reuse the 6-constant budget, no new constants, reducing overfitting risk. Test whether the double-gating on elongation over-slows late growth; if so keep only primary nucleation double-gated.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: shape_loss asymmetry and nmse simultaneously via physically grounded substrate depletion; strong robustness on small datasets due to unchanged constant count
tried_paradigms.cautions: The double (P-c) factor squares the deceleration near plateau which can make late-time fit too flat; verify plateau init ~0.95 so the curve still reaches the data max. Ensure derivative stays positive: all factors (P-c)>0 within [0,P] and squared constants guarantee sign. No singularities since no division by variable-dependent zeros. Keep monomer scaling *1e6 for conditioning.

tried_paradigms.idea: Introduce a state-cooperativity exponent on the growth term to control sigmoid asymmetry and lag sharpness (Richards/generalized-logistic capacity), directly targeting the 25% shape loss instead of the near-saturated NMSE.
tried_paradigms.description: Keep the source structure (seed + primary + elongation, inhibitor divisor) but replace the linear capacity (plateau - c) with a generalized Richards capacity that reshapes onset sharpness: expression = (seed_nuc + primary_nuc + elong) * (plateau - c) * (c + eps) / inhibitor_scale, i.e. multiply the rate by c itself so growth is autocatalytic-cooperative (dc/dt vanishes at c=0 unless seeded, producing a genuine lag then sharp rise). Concretely build growth = c[2]**2*monomer*concentration*(concentration + c[6]) to add a controllable quadratic self-acceleration, and keep capacity = plateau - concentration. The (concentration + small_seed) factor makes the derivative small at low c (lag phase) and peak near mid-c (sharp rise), matching amyloid asymmetry and the half-response timing the shape term measures. Use 7 constants: c0..c5 as now plus c6 (a small baseline seeding offset, squared to stay positive) so the M0=0 primary-nucleation channel still ignites. Feature scaling (1e6) unchanged. initial_values length must match constant count; set the c6 start near 0.05. This is fundamentally different from failed attempts because they modified the SOURCE/inhibitor terms while the shape mismatch lives in the STATE nonlinearity of the capacity/growth coupling.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: dataset_shape_loss (slope profile + half-response/onset timing), which is 25% of scored_loss and where failed source-term edits gained nothing; secondarily heldout_curve_shape_loss.
tried_paradigms.cautions: Do not add more than one new constant to avoid overfitting small datasets. Ensure (concentration + c6**2) stays strictly positive; c is normalized in [0,1] so no singularity. Keep the (plateau - c) capacity to preserve mass conservation and cd=0 limit. Only ONE evaluate_expression call, straight-line code, no Piecewise/Max. Verify initial_values has exactly 7 entries matching constant_symbols(7).

tried_paradigms.idea: Replace the multiplicative inhibitor rate-divisor with an inhibitor-shifted effective time/threshold inside the growth trigger, so cd suppresses the ONSET timing (half-time) rather than uniformly scaling the whole rate — targeting the timing component of the shape loss.
tried_paradigms.description: The shape loss explicitly compares half-response/onset timing; a pure rate divisor shifts the whole curve but cannot independently tune WHEN growth ignites vs how steep it is. Restructure so the inhibitor raises the autocatalytic ignition threshold: define effective_state = concentration / (1 + c[3]**2 * inhibitor) meaning inhibitor makes the aggregate 'feel' less advanced, delaying the point where autocatalysis takes off. Then growth = (c[0]**2*seed + c[1]**2*monomer + c[2]**2*monomer*effective_state) and expression = growth * (plateau - concentration). Here the capacity uses true concentration (mass conservation preserved, halts at plateau) but the autocatalytic drive uses the inhibitor-attenuated effective_state, so higher cd delays the sharp-rise phase and shifts t_half without flattening the final slope — decoupling timing from steepness. Use 5 constants (c0..c4 plateau), fewer than current, reducing overfit risk. inhibitor = x[3]*1e6, denominator 1+positive is singularity-free, cd=0 gives effective_state=concentration exactly (clean uninhibited limit). This differs from the failed 'time-delayed source coupling' because the delay is applied to the STATE feedback of autocatalysis, not as an additive onset shift to the source, giving a smooth self-consistent lag.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: half-response/onset timing portion of shape_loss and heldout extrapolation across cd values (heldout_curve_shape_loss), while keeping NMSE stable.
tried_paradigms.cautions: Denominator (1 + c3**2*inhibitor) must stay >0 (guaranteed). Keep plateau as bounded capacity so trajectory cannot overshoot 1. Do not combine with the elongation divisor to avoid double-counting inhibition. Exactly one evaluate_expression call; initial_values length = 5. Watch that fewer constants still fit high-cd curves — verify no dataset produces non-finite integration.

tried_paradigms.idea: Adopt a Gompertz-in-state formulation using log-capacity ln(plateau/(c+eps)) as the growth driver, giving intrinsic front-loaded asymmetry that matches amyloid lag-then-plateau shapes better than the symmetric logistic (plateau-c) capacity.
tried_paradigms.description: Amyloid aggregation curves are strongly asymmetric: long flat lag, rapid rise, gradual approach to plateau. The logistic (plateau-c) is symmetric about its midpoint and mismatches this, which the shape loss (slope profile) penalizes. Use a Gompertz capacity that is analytic and smooth: capacity_g = sp.log((plateau + c[5]**2) / (concentration + c[5]**2)) where c[5]**2 is a tiny positive regularizer keeping the log argument strictly positive over c in [0,1] and plateau near 0.92. Then expression = (c[0]**2*seed + c[1]**2*monomer + c[2]**2*monomer*concentration) * capacity_g / inhibitor_scale with inhibitor_scale = 1 + c[3]**2*inhibitor (simple saturating linear divisor, since the Gompertz already handles asymmetry and we avoid the failed Hill complexity). The log-ratio drives fast growth when c is small relative to plateau and smoothly vanishes as c->plateau, producing the characteristic Gompertz asymmetry and later t_half. Use 6 constants (c0 seed, c1 primary, c2 elong, c3 inhibitor, c4 plateau, c5 log-regularizer). Note: unlike the FAILED 'Gompertz-type growth' attempt which replaced the symmetric logistic capacity wholesale and likely destabilized fitting, here the log-ratio is regularized with c5**2 for numerical safety AND retains the additive nucleation channels and inhibitor divisor structure that already work — a minimal, well-conditioned change rather than a full reformulation.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: slope-profile component of shape_loss via asymmetric front-loaded kinetics; keep NMSE near current level; improve robustness on high-cd held-out curves.
tried_paradigms.cautions: sp.log argument must be strictly positive: since plateau (~0.92) and c<=~1, set c5 initial ~0.3 so c5**2~0.09 guarantees positivity even if c momentarily exceeds plateau during integration; monitor for non-finite values. This is the ONE risky operation — the c5**2 offset in both numerator and denominator of the ratio prevents log(0) and log(negative). Exactly one evaluate_expression call, straight-line, no conditionals. initial_values length = 6. Prefer this only if idea 1 and 2 stall, since log can complicate least-squares conditioning.

tried_paradigms.idea: Selective-channel inhibition: apply the saturable inhibitor factor ONLY to the elongation/autocatalytic channel (the timing-controlling term), leaving seed+primary nucleation uninhibited, and let the plateau depend weakly on cd
tried_paradigms.description: Keep the current 6-constant backbone but restructure WHICH channel the inhibitor divides. Physically, surface-binding inhibitors (Brichos-type) block fibril-surface elongation/secondary sites, not de-novo primary nucleation. So write rate = capacity * (seed_nuc + primary_nuc + elongation/inhibitor_scale) where only elongation = c2^2*monomer*concentration is divided by inhibitor_scale = 1 + c3^2*inhibitor^2/(c5^2+inhibitor). This decouples the LAG/onset control (elongation, which drives the autocatalytic burst and thus t_half) from the steady baseline drift (nucleation), directly attacking shape_loss timing without a proportional slowdown of the whole curve. Keep plateau = c4 (linear capacity P-c) unchanged since capacity-form changes already failed. Use the same 1e6 feature scaling and squared constants for non-negativity. This is 6 constants, same count, so no added overfitting. Return exactly one evaluate_expression call with initial_values roughly [0.5,0.05,0.5,1.0,0.92,0.3].
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: heldout_curve_shape_loss (onset/half-time timing) primarily, while preserving nmse; the decoupling should let the model delay the burst independently of the baseline
tried_paradigms.cautions: Denominator c5^2+inhibitor is strictly positive since inhibitor=cd*1e6>=0 and c5^2>0, so no singularity. cd=0 recovers inhibitor_scale=1 and full elongation. Ensure elongation is the ONLY divided term so nucleation baseline stays intact. Do not add extra constants.

tried_paradigms.idea: Reparameterize inhibitor as a dimensionless occupancy fraction theta=cd/(Kd+cd) that multiplicatively scales an effective growth rate down toward a floor, giving a bounded 0..1 suppression with a residual escape rate so high-dose curves still aggregate
tried_paradigms.description: Replace the unbounded divisor 1+k^2*cd^2/(Kd^2+cd) with a Langmuir occupancy suppression that is intrinsically bounded in [0,1]: theta = inhibitor/(c5^2 + inhibitor), and suppression = (1 - c3^2*theta) but guaranteed positive by using suppression = 1/(1 + c3^2*theta) which stays in (0,1] and asymptotes to a finite floor 1/(1+c3^2) at saturation. Then rate = suppression*capacity*(seed_nuc+primary_nuc+elongation). The physical principle: a finite site pool means delay saturates to a maximum (Langmuir occupancy), unlike the current cd^2/(Kd^2+cd) which grows linearly at large cd and can OVER-delay held-out high-cd curves. Because suppression is bounded below by a positive floor, the highest-dose extrapolated curves still reach the plateau, fixing the extrapolation failure mode flagged in the current code's own comments. Keep 6 constants, 1e6 scaling, squared non-negative constants, linear capacity P-c=c4-concentration.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: heldout_curve_nmse and extrapolation robustness on the highest-cd curves; a bounded suppression prevents the divergent over-delay the current template risks
tried_paradigms.cautions: 1/(1+c3^2*theta) is always in (0,1], smooth, singularity-free; theta in [0,1) since c5^2>0. cd=0 gives theta=0 and suppression=1 exactly. Verify the model does not collapse suppression to ~1 (i.e. c3^2 must be identifiable); if constants collapse, this is under-informative — but the floor structure is the target.

tried_paradigms.idea: Monomer-consuming closed-mass ODE: make the elongation driver use the DEPLETING free-monomer pool (plateau - c) itself as the reactant so growth is truly autocatalytic-then-self-limiting (c*(P-c) logistic core), with inhibitor slowing the autocatalytic rate constant, replacing the additive m0*c elongation with a mass-conserving logistic burst
tried_paradigms.description: The dominant weakness is the near-exponential (P-c) approach lacking a real sigmoidal lag-burst. Instead of ADDING a capacity factor (which failed), CHANGE the reactant of elongation to enforce closed mass conservation: as aggregate mass c grows, free monomer available for elongation is (P - c). So elongation = c2^2 * concentration * (plateau - concentration) is a genuine logistic autocatalytic term (Cohen-Knowles fibril mass ODE dM/dt ~ k*M*(m_tot - M)), which alone produces the sigmoidal lag-burst-plateau shape shape_loss rewards. Seed+primary nucleation seed the burst: rate = seed_nuc + primary_nuc + elongation/inhibitor_scale, where seed_nuc=c0^2*seed*(plateau-concentration) and primary_nuc=c1^2*monomer, and inhibitor_scale=1+c3^2*inhibitor^2/(c4^2+inhibitor). This removes the separate global (P-c) multiplier and instead bakes mass-conservation into each growth channel, giving true logistic shape. Use plateau as a fixed unit (c=normalized so P~1) OR one constant; keep total constants at 5-6. 1e6 scaling, squared constants.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: dataset_shape_loss via a genuine sigmoidal lag-burst-plateau from the logistic c*(P-c) core, plus nmse; targets the kinetic-shape penalty directly
tried_paradigms.cautions: The logistic term c*(P-c) is negative if c>P, which could push c above P then oscillate; guard by keeping plateau near 1 and ensuring seed_nuc also carries (P-c) so all growth halts at c=P. Ensure c(0) small initial_values so the lag phase exists (start below plateau). Watch odeint stiffness — logistic is smooth so integration is stable. Only ONE evaluate_expression call.
